---
title: "Group work"
format: html
---

## Summary

> In this group project, you will develop and analyze a realistic business analytics case, including data, modeling, and critical reflection.
  While many analytics courses rely on publicly available datasets—often centered on societal or general-purpose questions—this project takes a different approach.
  We focus on business-relevant contexts such as management, finance, operations, or digital platforms that are more directly aligned with your future professional environments.
  To achieve this, you will design and work with synthetic datasets that reflect realistic organizational processes and decision situations.
  This allows you not only to apply analytical methods, but also to engage with the upstream challenge of framing meaningful problems and constructing data that captures them.
  Also: downstream challenge of deployment (e.g., ethics, ...)
  Using suitable/reputable references to illustrate the prevalence/significance of the problem, and to show evidence that the proposed analytical approach and dataset is "typical" in practice

**Objectives**

- Design and analyze realistic business data scenarios
- Select and justify appropriate analytical methods
- Communicate results to technical and non-technical audiences
- Apply the CRISP-DM process as a scaffold
- Reflect on limitations and improvements


## Project Overview

### Group Formation

- The target group size is 4 students.
  We will select two groups with 5 students in a fair procedure.
  Groups with 5 students are expected to submit a more comprehensive case (+25%).
- Once you know your group, create or join the group in the [Canvas assignment]({{< var group_work.submission_url >}}).

<!--

- If you have formed a group, please **send me an email** with the names and email addresses of all group members.
- Groups of 4 will be **automatically accepted**.
- If you are not yet in a group, you can still participate: the **allocation of remaining students will take place during a session** (not after the deadline).
- If the total number of students does not divide evenly into groups of 4, some groups may have **3 or 5 members**, determined in a **fair and transparent way**.

- The group size depends on the difficulty level of the individual project (see project description).
- Each participant applies by e-mail for the desired projects. Alternatively, you can already form groups and apply as a group.
- In every case, please indicate three projects as:
  - Preference 1
  - Preference 2
  - Preference 3
- Based on the applications, the groups are arranged and projects are assigned.
-->

### Task

- Select a topic and business question. Topics could include:

  - Finance (e.g., credit default, fraud, customer lifetime value)
  - Marketing (e.g., churn, customer segmentation, campaign effectiveness)
  - Human Resources (e.g., attrition, promotion, team productivity)
  - Operations (e.g., demand forecasting, inventory optimization, supplier risk scoring)
  - Digital business (e.g., user engagement prediction, gig worker retention, pricing strategy simulation)

- Create a synthetic but realistic dataset to simulate internal company data.
  This could be complemented by synthetic or real external data.
- Prepare an analytical notebook, following the **CRISP-DM process**:

  1. Business Understanding *(What is the business context? What is the concrete question? Who are the stakeholders? Why does it matter?)*
  2. Data Understanding *(What does the dataset contain? How does it reflect a realistic business setting? What do you observe in the EDA?)*
  3. Data Preparation *(How is the data cleaned and transformed? What features are created? How is the data split?)*
  4. Modeling *(Which model is used? How is it trained? Why does it fit the problem?)*
  5. Evaluation *(Which metrics are used? How well does the model perform? What do the results mean in business terms?)*
  6. Deployment *(How would the model be used in practice? What inputs/outputs are required? What are key limitations or risks?)*

- Develop a report to reflect on the case (what students will learn/specific challenge; how it illustrates and complements materials covered in the lecture; what its simplyfying assumptions or limitations are; how it extends existing work; potential for improvement)

<!--
  - Select and apply analytical models and procedures covered in the lecture.
    Depending on the project, different focuses have to be set (e.g., for some projects more data preparation, for others more model optimization).
    Optional (challenge/better grades): include an advanced question (e.g., in data preparation; model refinement; evaluation; deployment)
-->

::: {.callout-note title="Notes"}

- Support key elements of your analysis with **reputable sources** (industry reports or academic literature), especially for:

  - problem relevance
  - typical data structures
  - common modeling approaches

- Simplification is acceptable. If a problem would typically require more advanced methods (e.g., reinforcement learning), you may use a simpler, well-justified approach.

- Optional extensions (for stronger projects) may include:

  - advanced data preparation or feature engineering
  - model comparison or refinement
  - robustness checks or extended evaluation
  - more detailed deployment considerations

:::


### Project Timeline

- Start: {{< var group_work.start_date >}}  
- Deadline: {{< var group_work.submission_date >}}

Note: GW: limited availability in the last week (2026-05-25 to 2026-06-01)

### Suggested Timeline

**Week 1 – Topic Selection**

  - Define and align on your project topic
  - Short proposal (≈1 page)
  - *Instructor check to ensure sufficient differentiation across groups*

**Weeks 2–4 – Iterative Development**

  - Develop and refine:

    - Problem framing & assumptions
    - Synthetic dataset and structure
    - Notebook (EDA + modeling)
  - Iterate between data design, analysis, and modeling
  - Gradually improve coherence and realism of the case

**Week 5 – Refinement**

  - Strengthen analysis and interpretation
  - Optional: add an advanced component (e.g., alternative model, robustness check, extension)

**Week 6 – Finalization**

  - Final report and code submission
  - Ensure clarity, reproducibility, and communication quality


```{mermaid}
gantt
    title Timeline
    dateFormat  YYYY-MM-DD

    section Setup
    Topic selection                  :active, 2026-04-14, 7d

    section Iterative Development
    Data, EDA, modeling (iterative)  : 2026-04-21, 21d

    section Refinement
    Refinement                       :2026-05-12, 7d

    section Finalization
    Final report & submission        :2026-05-19, 11d
```


### Deliverables

All necessary steps for the analysis should be performed in Python.


1. **Report (max 10 pages)**

   - Section 1: Case Package (explaining business context, problem statement)
   - Section 2: Structured explanation of approach


2. **Script for synthetic dataset**

  - Python script to generate the dataset

    TODO: give examples / link to my own synthetic dataset scripts

3. **Dataset**

   - CSV file(s) containing the simulated data (synthetic but realistic)

4. **Analytical Notebook (Python)**

   - Full CRISP-DM pipeline


### Evaluation Criteria

A max of 60 points can be earned, adding to the final grade.
Each group receives one grade.

<!--
  escalation procedure/how do we ensure fair contributions?
-->

| Category                              | Points | Criteria                                                                 |
| ------------------------------------- | ------ | ------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
| **A. Problem and case design**          | 15 pts | Relevance and clarity of business problem; Realism and quality of dataset design       |
| **B. Methodological rigor**           | 20 pts | Proper use of CRISP-DM; Justification of methods; Correct implementation |
| **C. Analysis quality**               | 15 pts | Insightfulness of results and interpretation             |
| **D. Communication**                  | 5 pts  | Clarity, structure, visualizations                                       |
| **E. Code quality** | 5 pts  | Clean, documented, runnable                                              |


Note: The applied methodology is more important for the evaluation than the absolute model performance.

**Synthetic Data Requirement**

- Must reflect realistic business data
- Could include noise, missing values, duplicates
- Must be documented in the script:

  - How it was generated
  - Assumptions (ideally with evidence)


**Difficulty Levels**

- **Standard**: basic pipeline + one model
- **Advanced**: focused extension in individual areas, such as specific data preparation challenge, interactive visualization, feature engineering, model refinement, advanced evaluation

<!--
Expectations (best grades: compelling motivation; instructive advanced analysis; strong contextualization and rationale)

Link examples? - ask for consent before.
-->

### AI policy

- Allowed for debugging, code explanations, documentation support
- Not allowed to generate solutions
- Students must explain and defend their work if needed.
  Everything must be validated.

<!--
TBD: share repository/directory and be prepared to answer questions on the analysis at any time (-> LLMs)
-->

### Consultation and Support

- See [Bookings]({{< var instructor.bookings_url >}}) and [teams]({{< var instructor.online_meeting_url >}}).

### Template

- Report template
- Notebook structure

### Submission

Via [Canvas assignment]({{< var group_work.submission_url >}}).


<!--
### C. Common Pitfalls Section (very effective pedagogically)

Example:

- Overfitting without validation
- Ignoring business interpretation
- Weak data generation
- “Model-first” instead of “problem-first”


Inspiration

- See [CS109A: Introduction to Data Science projects](https://harvard-iacs.github.io/2018-CS109A/pages/projects.html)
-->


::: {.callout-note title="Note"}
High-quality project work may contribute to future course development—for example, by extending teaching materials or informing teaching cases.
In such cases, we will discuss how contributions are acknowledged.
:::

<!--
TBD: announce the option for co-authorship? (mentioning student co-authored papers, 25%?) - if this is something that will be published, I will reach out and you will have the opportunity to become a coauthor.
-->
